title: Exponential Growth, Decay, and Logarithmic Scales · Guided Modeling Studio
summary: Model adoption, cooling, depreciation, and sound-level measurements. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of an exponential and logarithmic scenario lab while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Distinguish additive change from multiplicative growth and derive period factors.
Fit exponential growth and decay parameters with explicit initial conditions.
Use logarithms as inverses to solve for time, rate, or exponent.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.python:
"""applied-mathematics-intermediate cumulative model portfolio."""

from math import exp, log, sqrt


def reasonableness(value: float, lower: float, upper: float) -> bool:
    return lower <= value <= upper

steps.title: Produce a checked model result
steps.instruction: $1300 grows once per year at 5.5% for 2 years. Calculate the compounded value. Use the result to decide Growth Limitations for an exponential and logarithmic scenario lab.
steps.why: The Growth Limitations result for an exponential and logarithmic scenario lab is incomplete until its unit, assumptions, scale, and tolerance survive a reasonableness check.

content.text: Use P(1 + r)^t with P=1300, r=0.055, and t=2. Reconcile the result with this competency evidence: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Identify saturation, regime changes, and extrapolation risks in exponential models.

content.title: Before calculating
content.text: Before calculating Growth Limitations, predict the sign and approximate magnitude for an exponential and logarithmic scenario lab. Enter the requested quantity with a unit, then reconcile it with that prediction and A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Identify saturation, regime changes, and extrapolation risks in exponential models.

hints: Use P(1 + r)^t with P=1300, r=0.055, and t=2.

The accepted result is within 0.01 of the exact value and includes $ or dollars.
